3 min read
3 hours ago

--

A string can look broken for several completely different reasons. It may still be escaped for JSON, it may be written as Unicode code point labels, it may contain HTML entities, or the original bytes may have been decoded with the wrong character set.
Treating every case as “Unicode corruption” usually creates a second bug. A safer approach is to identify the representation first and repair the layer that produced it.
Start with the symptom, not the replacement
When you see \u0048\u0069, you are usually looking at an escaped representation of “Hi.” When you see U+0048 U+0069, you are looking at code point labels. When you see Hi, you are looking at HTML numeric entities. When you see cafÃ© instead of café, you are probably looking at UTF-8 bytes that were decoded as Windows-1252 or ISO-8859–1.
These examples can all display incorrect-looking text, but they require different fixes.

• Find the owner of the format
Ask which layer created the value:

• A JSON parser or serializer?

• An HTML template or CMS?

• A log viewer that escapes control characters?

• A database driver?

• A CSV import?

• A network client decoding response bytes?
The owner of the representation is usually where the correction belongs. A browser-side converter is useful for diagnosis, but production code should use the parser for the actual format.

• Parse valid JSON instead of replacing backslashes
If the input is a complete JSON document, use JSON.parse in JavaScript or json.loads in Python. The parser understands escaped quotes, control characters, backslashes, and UTF-16 surrogate pairs.
JavaScript example:
const payload = ‘{“message”:”\u0048\u0069",”emoji”:”\ud83d\ude00"}’;
const data = JSON.parse(payload);
console.log(data.message); // Hi
console.log(data.emoji); // 😀
A regular expression that only replaces \u followed by four hex digits is too narrow for arbitrary JSON. It can mishandle escaped backslashes and split characters outside the Basic Multilingual Plane.

• Check whether the string was decoded twice
Double decoding is common in systems with several boundaries: API gateway, application server, database, template, and browser. One layer decodes the text correctly, and a later layer assumes that escape sequences are still present.
Log both the value and its serialized form at each boundary. This helps you distinguish a real backslash from a display tool escaping the value for readability.

• Validate surrogate pairs
Many emoji and historic scripts do not fit in one UTF-16 code unit. JSON may represent them with a high surrogate followed by a low surrogate. The pair \uD83D\uDE00 represents 😀.
A valid JSON parser combines the pair. A custom decoder should validate the pair and report isolated surrogates instead of silently discarding them.

• Separate escaping from encoding
Escaping changes how characters are represented inside another format. Encoding maps characters to bytes.
Mojibake is usually an encoding-boundary problem. For example, the UTF-8 bytes for “é” are C3 A9. If those bytes are interpreted as Windows-1252, the result becomes “Ã©.” No amount of \uXXXX replacement will solve the underlying byte-decoding mistake.
To investigate mojibake, capture the original bytes if possible, identify the declared Content-Type and charset, check database and CSV import settings, and confirm that every boundary agrees on UTF-8.

• Use small test cases
Create a table of known inputs and expected outputs:

• Basic Latin: \u0048\u0069 → Hi

• HTML entity: ☃ → ☃

• Supplementary character: \uD83D\uDE00 → 😀

• Literal backslash: \\u0041 should remain a visible escape in the right context

• Malformed escape: \u12G4 should produce a clear error

• Mojibake sample: cafÃ© should be investigated as an encoding mismatch
Run these cases through the same path as production data. Unit tests at the parser boundary are more valuable than a global text-cleanup function applied at the end.

• Protect private data while debugging
Logs and API payloads can contain credentials, customer names, or internal identifiers. Prefer local tools and reduced test strings. Remove secrets before pasting any payload into a third-party service.
My final checklist
Before changing code, I verify:

• The exact representation is known.

• The correct parser owns the conversion.

• The value has not already been decoded.

• Surrogate pairs are handled.

• Malformed input is reported.

• The byte encoding is verified separately.

• Private data stays local.
For a quick browser-side comparison, ASCII to Unicode can inspect JSON escapes, HTML entities, U+ code points, hex escapes, and common mojibake without uploading the pasted text: https://asciitounicode.com/
The converter is most useful as a diagnostic aid. Once the representation is identified, fix the parser, serializer, or encoding boundary that actually owns the data.
